keyEvents.impact: Decade of upheaval forged revolutionary nationalism and the principle of non-intervention
Nationalized petroleum became a sacred symbol of economic sovereignty
Seven decades of one-party rule created a strong presidency and corporatist state machinery
Economy bound irrevocably to the United States; manufacturing export boom and deep interdependence

keyEvents.event: PRI loses presidency to Vicente Fox
keyEvents.impact: Peaceful democratic alternation ended one-party rule after 71 years
Military deployment against cartels unleashed enduring violence claiming hundreds of thousands of lives
Left-populist realignment, expanded military role in civilian tasks, and friction with judicial institutions

history.narrative: Bound to the United States by trade, migration, and geography yet fiercely protective of its sovereignty, Mexico practices a non-interventionist diplomacy rooted in revolutionary nationalism. Its central struggle is internal: cartel violence and weak rule of law sap the state, while the US relationship must be managed without appearing subordinate.

redLines.description: Unilateral US military intervention on Mexican soil against cartels
US withdrawal from USMCA or punitive blanket tariffs on Mexican exports
Foreign interference in Mexican elections or energy sovereignty

internalDivisions.name: Morena populism versus opposition over institutions and judiciary

goals.description: Preserve USMCA trade access and capture nearshoring investment
Contain cartel violence and rebuild state authority in contested regions
Defend national sovereignty and non-intervention against US pressure
Lead Latin American diplomacy as a bridge between North and South
